coredata.dc:title: pH and solvent induced discoloration behavior of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiyao
Sun, Lili
Guo, Xiangjun
Zhang, Hongmei
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: Green, blue and pink fluorescent carbon dots (o/m/p-CDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method with three isomers (o/m/p) of phenylenediamine as nitrogen and carbon sources and ethanol as solvent. The obtained carbon dots exhibited different luminescence behaviors, and the luminescence behaviors were affected by solvent and pH. More interestingly, m-CDs exhibited obvious discoloration behavior under different pH conditions and had excellent linear relationship under acidic and basic conditions, respectively. Furthermore, we combined m-CDs with test paper, PVA film, and cotton fabric, respectively, to prepare composites for visual pH detection. The as-prepared composite material has excellent visual detection effect for a small amount of test sample (<5 μL) in the fine pH range (0.5 unit), and could be applied to trace pH detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor
PH detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of o-CDs, m-CDs and p-CDs
Preparation of pH-responsive test papers, PVA films and cotton fabric based on m-CDs
Characterization of o-CDs, m-CDs and p-CDs
Optical properties of o-CDs, m-CDs and p-CDs
Solid-state fluorescent composite materials based on o/m/p-CDs as pH sensors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco Textiles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco Textiles ( Qingdao University ) (No. ZKT41 , No. ZFZ201815 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A-C) TEM and HR-TEM of o/m/p-CDs, respectively, (D) FT-IR, (E) XPS spectrum and (F) fluorescence lifetime of o/m/p-CDs.

The corresponding UV–vis, FL emission, FL excitation spectra and CIE coordinates with a normalization of (A-C) o-CDs, (D-F) m-CDs and (G-I) p-CDs. Insets of (A, D, G) showed the digital photos of o/m/p-CDs under UV light at 365 nm.

(A) FL emission spectra of m-CDs at different pH. (B) Photographs of m-CDs at different pH under 365 nm UV light. (C) Liner relationship between pH and max emission wavelength. (D) Fluorescence reversible response against pH varied repeatedly between 2.5 and 11.0. λex/λem were 360/500 nm.

(A) Photos of o-CDs/PVA, m-CDs/PVA and p-CDs/PVA at dry state and wet state. (B) Reversible cyclic discoloration of m-CDs/PVA at dry and wet states. FL emission spectra of m-CDs/PVA at dry (C) and wet (D) states. (E)Photos of o-CDs/fabric, m-CDs/fabric and p-CDs/fabric. (F) Maximum emission spectra of o-CDs/fabric, m-CDs/fabric and p-CDs/fabric. (G) Response photos of fluorescent m-CDs test papers, PVA films and cotton fabric to buffer solutions of different pH, respectively. (H) Scheme diagram of m-CDs involved in fluorescence transition in acid and base conditions.
Diagram of synthesis and sensing detection process.

head.ce:title: pH and solvent induced discoloration behavior of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green, blue and pink fluorescent carbon dots (o/m/p-CDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method with three isomers (o/m/p) of phenylenediamine as nitrogen and carbon sources and ethanol as solvent. The obtained carbon dots exhibited different luminescence behaviors, and the luminescence behaviors were affected by solvent and pH. More interestingly, m-CDs exhibited obvious discoloration behavior under different pH conditions and had excellent linear relationship under acidic and basic conditions, respectively. Furthermore, we combined m-CDs with test paper, PVA film, and cotton fabric, respectively, to prepare composites for visual pH detection. The as-prepared composite material has excellent visual detection effect for a small amount of test sample (<5 μL) in the fine pH range (0.5 unit), and could be applied to trace pH detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) has aroused extensive research interest due to its diversified synthesis methods, adjustable luminescence, and applicability in a wide range of fields including bioimaging [1], sensing [2], and light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [3]. So far, many reports have applied CDs in pH detection. For example, Wang et al. synthesized CDs using dehydrated shiitake mushrooms as the sole carbon source [4]. The prepared CDs displayed unique pH sensitivity in the pH range of 4.0–8.0 and successfully applied to pH detection in living cells and heme. Wang et al. creatively used double-emitting CDs for pH detection, which employing the ratio of the two intensities at 447 and 581 nm (RI447/I581) as the signal for the determinations with greater precision [5]. Gan et al. successfully synthesized blue carbon quantum dots using folic acid and niacin as raw materials to detect ascorbic acid and nitrite, and also showed good sensitivity to different pH values, showing their potential for pH sensing [6]. It must be pointed out that although the detection results of these probes were good enough, its signal output depended heavily on the fluorescence intensity change of a single wavelength [6,7], which was not conducive to visual detection. Therefore, it is essential to develop a pH-responsive fluorescent probe with color changes [8].

Phenylenediamine is the simplest aromatic diamine, and its three isomer forms （o/m/p）are mostly used in the synthesis of azo dyes and polymers. Phenylenediamine has a conjugated structure based on the benzene ring, which makes it an excellent condition for nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots. At present, there are many studies on the use of phenylenediamine to synthesize a variety of multicolor carbon dots. Jiang et al. used p-phenylenediamine as a precursor to successfully synthesize red green and blue fluorescent carbon dots and applied them to live cell imaging [9]. Cai et al. prepared three-color emission CDs with phenylenediamine as carbon sources and applied them to light-emitting diodes [10]. Barhum et al. successfully synthesized high quantum yield multicolor CDs to detect metal ions with picomolar sensitivity [11]. Although these studies have optimized the preparation and luminescence effects of CDs, there still have great potential in pH detection application. In this work, green (o-CDs), blue (m-CDs), and pink (p-CDs) fluorescent CDs were synthesized using three isomers (o/m/p) of phenylenediamine as the carbon and nitrogen sources respectively in ethanol solvent. The pH and solvent response properties of the prepared CDs were explored. Moreover, the blue fluorescent CDs (m-CDs) were combined with test paper, PVA film and cotton fabric to form solid fluorescent composite materials as visible pH fluorescence detection probes in real time. Scheme 1 displayed the schematic diagram of preparation and sensing.

P-phenylenediamine was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and ethanol, polyvinyl alcohol was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). Acid-base buffer solution purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). Cotton fabric was purchased from Fruida company. (Shandong, China). Deionized water is used throughout the experiment. NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2 ZnCl2, BaCl2, HgS, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, KBr, KI, NaNO2, NaNO3, Na3PO4, Na2S, Na2SO4, Na2SiO4 was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were performed using JEM-1200 EX (Japan, JEOL) transmission electron microscope. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was displayed with a DX-2700 X-ray diffractometer (XRD), and the measurement was performed with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) in the 2θ range of 0°/5–90°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed on a spectrometer (NICO-LET5700) by the KBr tablet method, with a scanning range of 400–4000 cm−1 and a resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Fluorescence (FL) measurement was performed on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Kyoto, Japan), with a scanning range of 300–700 nm. UV–vis spectroscopy was performed on a TU 1901 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Persee, Beijing, China) from 200 to 800 nm. The fluorescence decay curve was performed with Edinburgh FLS980.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of o-CDs, m-CDs and p-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.002 g of o/m/p-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol to obtain a homogeneous solution after 10 min sonication. Subsequently, the obtained homogeneous solution was transferred into 20 mL Teflon autoclave for 10 h reaction at 180 °C. The crude products were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min and filtered through 0.22 µm pore size filter membrane to obtain clarified carbon dots solution. Finally, the obtained transparent three kinds of liquid were named o-CDs, m-CDs, p-CDs (o/m/p-CDs) and stored at 4 °C for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pH-responsive test papers, PVA films and cotton fabric based on m-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mL of o/m/p-CDs were dropped uniformly on the filter paper and dried to prepare test papers containing CDs. In addition, 4 mL of o/m/p-CDs was mixed in 15 mL PVA solution (2.5%), then vacuum dried to obtain a fluorescent PVA film. Finally, 10 cm × 5 cm cotton fabric was immersed in 2 mL o/m/p-CDs respectively, fully absorbed o/m/p-CDs, dried and divide into 1 cm × 1 cm for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of o-CDs, m-CDs and p-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of o/m/p-CDs were observed with TEM and shown in Fig. 1. The o-CDs and m-CDs all showed uniform dispersion (Fig. 1A& B), while p-CDs had a certain degree of aggregation (Fig. 1C). The possible reason was that the para-position structure of p-phenylenediamine made the synthesized p-CDs easier to polymerize due to azo reaction. Therefore, the cause of the different luminescence behavior of o/m/p-CDs had no relationship with the particle size, while may be due to other influential factors. In addition, the HR-TEM image of o/m/p-CDs showed obvious lattice fringes (0.21 nm), corresponding to the (002) of graphite. To further obtain the structure and functional groups composition of o/m/p-CDs, FT-IR and XRD were tested. FT-IR demonstrated o/m/p-CDs contained approximately the same functional groups with only a few differences. Clearly, a distinct hydroxyl peak appeared at 3400 cm−1 [12]. Meanwhile, a weaker peak existed at 2950 cm−1, indicating the presence of methylene [13]. Although the presence of the amide carbonyl group was demonstrated at 1660 cm−1 [14,15], the difference was that C-N appeared at 1417 cm−1 in o-CDs [15,16] and CN at 1508 cm−1 in p-CDs [17]. These two peaks did not exist in m-CDs (Fig. 1D). XRD revealed that a broad reflection was observed at around 2θ= 21.12°, ascribing graphitic structure (002) of carbon, which indicates a consistent crystallographic structure of the o/m/p-CDs (Fig. S1) [18].

In addition, the chemical composition of o-CDs, m-CDs and p-CDs was determined by XPS. XPS spectra showed that the nitrogen content increased gradually with the gradual increase of emission wavelength (Fig. 1E). The blue-emitting m-CDs expressed the lowest nitrogen content of 0.98%. Meanwhile, the nitrogen content of green-emitting o-CDs was 2.15%. Differently, the red p-CDs with the longest emission wavelength had the highest nitrogen content of 2.34%. These differences were thought to be at least partly responsible for the different PL characteristics of three CDs, but more in-depth studies are needed to address this issue [19–21]. Increased nitrogen content in carbon core exhibited a red-shifted emission by controlling sp2 domains, enhancing heteroatom incorporation and surface charge [22]. The high resolution deconvoluted spectra of each XPS peak were shown in Fig. S2A–C. 282.90 eV and 285.0 eV for C-N [20,23], 284.5 eV for C-C/CC [24], 285.9 eV for C-N/CO [25] were presented in o-CDs, respectively. Three peaks of m-CDs at 282.5 eV, 284.6 eV and 286.3 eV corresponded to C-N [23], C-C/CC [26] and C-N/C-O [27], respectively. Similarly, two peaks of p-CDs appeared at 282.5 eV and 285.0 eV, corresponding to C-N[13] and C-C/CC[24], respectively (Fig. S2A). The N1s spectra of o/m/p-CDs was almost the same and could be deconvoluted into two bands at 397.2 eV (imidazolic N-H group) and 398.7 eV (N-H group) (Fig. S2B) [28,29]. The O1s spectra can be divided into two peaks located at 531.5 eV and 532.2 eV, which belonged to C-O [30] and C-OH/C-O-C (Fig. S2C) [31–33]. XPS characterization showed that o/m/p-CDs formed abundant nitrogen doping and surface functional groups, and the slight difference in functional groups was one of important reasons for the luminescence specificity of three CDs.

In addition, time-resolved fluorescence decays were determined and the obtained lifetime decay curves were fitted well by a double-exponential function y(t) with lifetimes ι1 and ι2 (Eq. (1)) and the average fluorescence lifetimes (τave) was calculated according to Eq. (2).

where τ 1 and τ 2 represent the decay time constants, and B1 and B2 are the pre-exponential factors for the time resolved decay lifetime of τ1 and τ2, respectively. τ
ave of o/m/p-CDs were 19.53 ns, 3.31 ns and 9.28 ns, respectively (Fig. 1F) [34]. The marked difference of the fluorescence lifetime may be related to the different surface chemical environment of o/m/p-CDs. Moreover, different degrees of hydrogen bond interaction between o/m/p-CDs and ethanol may also led to the difference of the fluorescence lifetimes [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of o-CDs, m-CDs and p-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of o/m/p-CDs were also investigated using UV–vis and FL spectroscopy. The o/m/p-CDs exhibited distinct multicolor tunable emissions. UV-Vis spectra displayed that a typical π-π * transition appeared at the 250 nm in o-CDs, which originated from sp2 hybridization of carbon[6]. In addition, the n- π * transition led to an absorption peak of CO at 375 nm, which was usually associated with the doping of carbon skeleton and surface functional groups ( Fig. 2A) [36]. Fig. 2B showed the full emission spectra of o-CDs under different excitation conditions. The o-CDs presented a maximum emission wavelength of 525 nm at around 420 nm excitation. The coordinates of the maximum emission in CIE were (0.29, 0.60), indicating that o-CDs were distinct green emission (Fig. 2C). In addition, m-CDs presented an intense UV absorption peak at 220 nm, which may be caused by the presence of the K absorption band of conjugated double bond system. Meanwhile, the maximum emission wavelength was blue-shifted to 475 nm when excited at around 390 nm (Fig. 2D). Fig. 2E showed the full emission spectra of m-CDs. The maximum excitation and maximum emission wavelengths of m-CDs were 390 nm and 475 nm, respectively, with a slight excitation-dependent behavior appeared. The coordinates of the maximum emission in CIE were (0.16, 0.25), indicating that m-CDs were distinct blue emission (Fig. 2F). Typical single emission behaviors were observed in o/m-CDs with no apparent excitation-dependent behavior.

Different from single-peak emission of o/m-CDs, p-CDs showed obvious double-peak emission. The p-CDs represented a weaker excitation peak at 360 nm and a stronger excitation peak at 500 nm. These two excitation peaks led to obvious double-peak emission behavior. UV spectra of p-CDs exhibited a broad peak around 250 nm and 280 nm contributing to the π-π * and n-π * transition of carbonyl group on the aromatic backbone of carbon (Fig. 2G) [18]. Fig. 2H showed that p-CDs performed two emission peaks at 430 nm and 595 nm, respectively, and the maximum intensity of both emission peaks were almost the same. With the gradual increase of excitation wavelength, the intensity of the p-CDs emission peak at 430 nm gradually decreased, while the intensity of the emission peak at 595 nm gradually increased. The CIE coordinates of Fig. 2I indicated that the fluorescence of p-CDs shifted from pink to orange-yellow as the excitation wavelength increased. It was worth noting that the dual-emission of p-CDs exhibited excitation independent behavior. It can be seen that two different emission peaks always existed under different excitation conditions. However, only the intensities rather position of two emission peaks vary with excitation wavelength.

According to the synergistic luminescence mechanism of carbon dots (molecule state and carbon-core state) in the previous report [36], the luminescence of CDs was also affected under different pH conditions. As shown in Fig. S3, both o-CDs and p-CDs showed obvious quenching effect in acid/alkali condition, while m-CDs maintained the original blue luminescence in alkaline and changed to green under acidic condition. Thus, in this work, the pH of m-CDs was finely modulated with hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide, and the changes of its luminescent properties were measured. Fig. 3A displayed the fluorescence spectral changes of m-CDs with pH, which demonstrates that m-CDs possess significant pH-dependent behavior. With the pH increase from 6.45 to 0.30, the emission center gradually red-shifted to 500 nm, showing distinct green fluorescence. Simultaneously, the fluorescence intensity obviously increased gradually. On the contrary, a new peak appeared at 430 nm concomitantly and the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the increase of pH values from pH 6.45–11.15. This response to pH was accompanied by a significant color change from green to blue observed under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3B).

It is noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity of m-CDs and pH values presented favorable linearity in the pH range of 0.30–6.45 and 6.45–11.15, respectively (Fig. 3C). The corresponding linear regression equation was calculated as: acidic condition (pH=0.30–6.45): y = 14933–1843x (R2 =0.9905) and alkaline condition (pH=6.45–11.15): y = 2809 + 9017x (R2 =0.9827). More interestingly, when instrumentation errors and operational errors were neglected, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared m-CDs was reversible during continuous at least ten cycles of acidification and alkalization between pH= 2.5 and pH= 11.0 (Fig. 3D), implying that m-CDs possess good cycling stability and a highly reversible response to pH. Meanwhile, the visual fluorescent color of the solution varied between green (pH 2.5) and blue (pH 11.0). Therefore, m-CDs can be used as a sensitive pH-reversible response fluorescent probe to detect pH fluctuations in real time.

To demonstrate the possibility of m-CDs in practical applications, the sensitivities of metal cations and anions were tested. As shown in Fig. S4, after adding 300 μM common metal ions and anions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Hg2+, CO3 2-, Cl-, Br-, I-, HCO3 -, NO2 -, NO3 -, PO4 3-, S2-, SO4 2-, SiO4 2-) to m-CDs respectively, the relative fluorescence intensity of m-CDs did not change significantly. Thus, we can exclude typical ions from interfering with the practical application of m-CDs.

The surface of CDs contained abundant functional groups, such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups, etc. which play different effects on the luminescence properties of the CDs [11,37]. In addition, the zeta potential of the original m-CDs was 2.93 mV. With the increase of pH from 1 to 14, the zeta potential gradually decreased from 12.33 mV to − 3.08 mV, which was due to the protonation and deprotonation of functional groups such as amino and carboxyl groups on the surface, thereby lead to the response of m-CDs to different pH. Therefore, the acid-base tuning fluorescence of m-CDs was mainly caused by protonation and deprotonation [36]. Moreover, at lower pH of acidic condition, H-bond binding induces the strong vibration coupling of –OH and –NH2 functional groups, which broadens the energy level and enhances the conformational rigidity of the CDs, resulting in the increase of fluorescent intensity of m-CDs [38]. On the other hand, at higher pH of alkaline condition, We inferred that the fluorescence enhancement of m-CDs may be owing to the negatively charged carboxylate on the surface of m-CDs protective shell derived from, which induces a protective layer and limits the nonradiative recombination rate of the m-CDs photoexcited state, resulting in significant fluorescence enhancement [39]. In addition, the different luminescence behaviors of o/m/p-CDs may be due to the different conjugated sp2 structure in the carbon core, the pH value affects the chemical groups (such as amino and carboxyl), further affects the electronic structure of the conjugated system in the carbon core [37].

In addition, discoloration caused by solvent effect was also considered. As shown in Fig. S5A, o-CDs exhibited obvious green fluorescence in ethanol solution, while turned to khaki in aqueous solution. Likewise, m-CDs changed from blue in ethanol to cyan in aqueous solution and p-CDs changed from pink in ethanol to dark red in aqueous solution. In order to further study the influence of solvent on the luminescence behavior of o/m/p-CDs, different ratios of aqueous ethanol were added to the same amounts of the dried o/m/p-CDs. under different water content, o-CDs gradually changed from green to yellow-green (Fig. S5B). It was worth noting that dried o-CDs was almost insoluble in 100% pure water and could not restore fluorescence. The difference was that m-CDs changed from blue to cyan and still had excellent solubility at 100% water (Fig. S5C). In addition, when the water content gradually increased, p-CDs also changed from pink to red, and turned to dark red when the water content reached 100% (Fig. S5D). According to previous reports, solvent with different polarity can cause changes in the emission behavior of CDs. Typically, solvatochromism effects were attributed to intramolecular charge transfer. The surface groups of CDs were enriched with oxygen and nitrogen substructures, which increased the carrier density and induce electron transfer to the surface charge [40–42]. Furthermore, the structures of the abundant groups on the surfaces of CDs as excited-state fluorescence centers was affected by the solvent dipole moment[43]. Therefore, greater solvent polarity will have a more severe effect and led to easier reduction of the luminous energy band gap, which will result in a redshift of the FL emission wavelength of the CDs [43,44]. Thus, in high-polarity solvents (such as water), the process of inducing the charge transfer in the photophysical led to the solvatochromism of CDs [45–47]. Compared with o/m/p-CDs in ethanol solvent, o/m/p-CDs in water possess a large red shift of the emission wavelength. The solvatochromism of the o/m/p-CDs may be due to the change of molecular chromophore caused by the polarity of the solvents and hydrogen bonds [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid-state fluorescent composite materials based on o/m/p-CDs as pH sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Up to now, our work had successfully found m-CDs with significant pH response and excellent linearity. Therefore, we consider combining it with solid-state materials to obtain a simpler and faster detection method. So far, Pei Yang et al. tried to combine carbon quantum dots with medical gauze, and used visually visible discoloration behavior to detect the pH of the wound [49]. Although their experiments had achieved good results, there were still many shortcomings. For example, in their work, the carbon dots showed a transition from yellow in neutral or alkaline state to orange luminescence in acidic conditions. Therefore, the degree of discrimination was not obvious under naked eyes due to similar fluorescence colors. What more, only the orange and yellow luminescence transition could be observed under 254 nm excitation, while it was almost indistinguishable under 365 nm excitation condition. Therefore, we try to combine o/m/p-CDs with PVA to obtain solid fluorescent PVA films, which could overcome the above shortcomings. The prepared PVA fluorescent film succeeded in getting rid of the influence of water after it was completely dried and finally showed the same luminescence behavior as in ethanol. That is, PVA films consist of o/m/p-CDs showed green, blue and pink fluorescence emission, respectively. Interestingly, PVA films were affected by the solvent and showed the same fluorescence emission as in water when they were re-wet, ( Fig. 4A). In addition, the fluorescent PVA film constructed from m-CDs had reversibility of different luminescence behaviors in dry and wet conditions (Fig. 4B). The excitation emission spectra of m-CDs/PVA at dry and wet state were given in Fig. 4C&D and Fig. S6, which showed significantly difference. The m-CDs/PVA at the dry state presented a maximum emission wavelength of 415 nm under 360 nm excitation, while the maximum emission wavelength of m-CDs/PVA at the wet state was red-shifted to 480 nm excited by 390 nm. At the same time, the fluorescent cotton fabrics embedded with o/m/p-CDs were prepared. It can be seen that the prepared fluorescent cotton fabrics expressed almost similar max emission wavelength to o/m/p-CDs in ethanol (Fig. 4E&F). Fig. S7A-C presented the full emission spectra of o/m/p-CDs/fabrics under different wavelengths excitation, which indicated that the optical properties of fluorescence cotton fabrics showed no difference between carbon quantum dots.

Thus, the luminescence stability of m-CDs on different load materials indicated that the solid-state fluorescent composites can also be used for pH detection. We embedded m-CDs with test paper, PVA film and cotton fabric to prepare the solid-state fluorescent composites. 5 μL buffer solutions was dropped (pH= 5.5/6/6.5/7/7.5/8) on fluorescent m-CDs/test paper. As shown in Fig. 4G, the test paper showed a clear change from green to cyan, then to dark blue as the pH gradually increased. This indicated that the fluorescent test paper prepared by m-CDs had good visibility. At the same process, the m-CDs were combined with PVA film and cotton fabric, respectively. The detection of pH was achieved under the same conditions. The m-CDs/PVA and m-CDs/fibric also showed significant visual discoloration in the pH range of 5/5.5/6/6.5/7/7.5/8. In conclusion, the results showed that the three solid-state fluorescent detection materials could realize very small sample (＜0.5 μL) test under the division of weak acid and fine pH range (0.5 units). Also, the schematic diagram of m-CDs involved in fluorescence transition in acid and base conditions was given in Fig. 4H.

Additionally, the stability of m-CDs/fabric was also tested. Fig. S8A showed that the relative fluorescence intensity was only slightly reduced under 4 h of UV light irradiation, while it still performed good stability when it was placed at a temperature range of 10 ℃− 70 ℃ (Fig. S8B) and daily conditions for 5/10/15/20 days (Fig. S8C). the excellent stability demonstrated that the prepared solid fluorescent materials was suitable for practical application.

The above test results showed that our solid-state fluorescent material for pH detection had excellent visual visibility. Moreover, the preparation process and the detection process were simple and quick with high sensitivity. In addition, compared to similar pH sensors, our pH sensors displayed a good linear range over the full pH range of 1–14. Simultaneously, the detected pH unit was narrowed down to 0.5. Moreover, unlike other sensors based on single fluorescence intensity change, m-CDs not only had fluorescence intensity change with good linear relationship, but also possessed visual color-changing effects. Therefore, the pH sensors constructed from m-CDs had a comparable or even better performance over other sensors of the same type (Table S1).

Using three isomers of phenylenediamine and ethanol as nitrogen and carbon sources, blue, yellow-green and pink fluorescent o-CDs, m-CDs and p-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. In summary, m-CDs showed reversible color behavior under different pH conditions with a good linear range. Moreover, o/m/p-CDs also showed similar discoloration behavior in aqueous solution and ethanol. The pH discoloration and lyotropic discoloration of m-CDs were discussed. Then m-CDs were compounded with test paper, PVA film and cotton fabric. The prepared composite material exhibited green fluorescence under weakly acidic conditions and blue luminescence under alkaline conditions. It was possible to visually detect a finer pH (0.5 unit) in a small amount of test sample (5 μL) through color changes.

The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work. We declared that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted. The contents of this manuscript have not been copyrighted or published previously. The contents of this manuscript are not now under consideration for publication elsewhere.
I am one author signing on behalf of all co-authors of this manuscript, and attesting to the above.

Jiyao Xu: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Lili Sun: Investigation. Xiangjun Guo: Investigation. Hongmei Zhang: Investigation. Xihui Zhao: Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco Textiles (Qingdao University) (No. ZKT41, No. ZFZ201815).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129261.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
